title: Data classes and value modeling

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Data classes and value modeling; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Standard Library; Python 3.14; dataclasses, collections.abc, and data-model-supporting library documentation; atomic topic: Data classes and value modeling
sources.claim: A Python data class generates selected initialization, representation, and comparison methods from annotated fields, making transparent value models concise while leaving validation and mutability policy explicit.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Python data class generates selected initialization, representation, and comparison methods from annotated fields, making transparent value models concise while leaving validation and mutability policy explicit.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A frozen Point dataclass stores two floats and validates finiteness in __post_init__, providing transparent field-based equality. Your goal is to choose and implement an appropriate data class or regular class for a domain model. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Scope and closures; Modules and imports; Classes, instances and attributes; Mutability, aliases and copying. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Python data class generates selected initialization, representation, and comparison methods from annotated fields, making transparent value models concise while leaving validation and mutability policy explicit. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Data classes are not runtime type validators, mutable defaults need default_factory, frozen is not deep immutability, ordering needs coherent fields, and identity-rich behavior may suit a regular class. The learner must reject this documented misconception: @dataclass validates annotations and creates a fresh copy of any default list for every instance.

includes: Use data classes for transparent values and custom classes when invariants or behavior require them.
Choose and implement an appropriate data class or regular class for a domain model.
excludes: Data classes are not runtime type validators, mutable defaults need default_factory, frozen is not deep immutability, ordering needs coherent fields, and identity-rich behavior may suit a regular class.

outcomes: Choose and implement an appropriate data class or regular class for a domain model.

examples.title: Data classes and value modeling: direct language case
examples.situation: A frozen Point dataclass stores two floats and validates finiteness in __post_init__, providing transparent field-based equality.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A frozen Point dataclass stores two floats and validates finiteness in __post_init__, providing transparent field-based equality.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Dataclass annotations drive generated methods rather than runtime validation; use default_factory for per-instance mutable defaults and validate invariants explicitly.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Dataclass annotations drive generated methods rather than runtime validation; use default_factory for per-instance mutable defaults and validate invariants explicitly.

examples.title: Data classes and value modeling: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A list field uses field(default_factory=list) so each instance owns a distinct collection.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A list field uses field(default_factory=list) so each instance owns a distinct collection.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Data classes are not runtime type validators, mutable defaults need default_factory, frozen is not deep immutability, ordering needs coherent fields, and identity-rich behavior may suit a regular class.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: @dataclass validates annotations and creates a fresh copy of any default list for every instance.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Choose and implement an appropriate data class or regular class for a domain model.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: @dataclass validates annotations and creates a fresh copy of any default list for every instance.
misconceptions.correction: Dataclass annotations drive generated methods rather than runtime validation; use default_factory for per-instance mutable defaults and validate invariants explicitly.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: @dataclass validates annotations and creates a fresh copy of any default list for every instance.

analogy.example: a family of service records whose instance state, generated value methods, inheritance order, and protocols define observable behavior can introduce Data classes and value modeling, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: classes, data classes, composition, inheritance, and special methods
limitations: descriptor lookup, MRO, hashing, NotImplemented, and shallow frozen state do not map cleanly to physical records
